skillName: Area of rectangles, parallelograms, triangles and compound shapes
misconceptionSummary: Students add the areas of sub-shapes in a compound figure but include an overlapping region twice.

lines.text: L-shape split into two rectangles A and B
Student: Area = A + B (using the full dimensions of each)  ✗
Overlapping corner counted in both A and B

steps.narration: When splitting a compound shape, the split must produce non-overlapping parts. If the rectangles are drawn with full dimensions that overlap, the corner region gets counted twice — giving an answer that is too large.

visuals.ruleText: Split the compound shape so every part is non-overlapping — each square centimetre belongs to exactly one region
visuals.subText: Draw the dividing line inside the shape, not extending across it

steps.narration: The dividing line must run cleanly through the interior, creating two regions with no overlap. Adjust the dimension of each region to reflect only its actual area — not the full span of the original shape.

lines.text: Split into: Rectangle A: 10×5, Rectangle B: 6×3

steps.narration: One valid split: a ten by five rectangle for the bottom section and a six by three rectangle for the top step. The dimensions are adjusted so there is no overlap. Fifty plus eighteen is sixty-eight square centimetres.

visuals.label: Non-overlapping split → calculate each part → add

steps.narration: Sixty-eight square centimetres. Split cleanly, adjust dimensions, add. Every part of the shape is counted exactly once.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: splitting a compound shape into rectangles with overlapping dimensions, counting a corner region twice. Ensure the split produces non-overlapping regions — adjust dimensions accordingly.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Each square centimetre must belong to exactly one region. Check your split produces non-overlapping parts — if dimensions overlap, a region is being counted twice.
